metadata.title: Legitimacy Auditor — Pragmatic, Moral, Cognitive (Suchman 1995)
metadata.note: CLI 10-beat spine: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 SUMMARY · B08 NEXT STEPS · B09 outro.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Mark Suchman's 1995 framework distinguishes three types of organizational legitimacy: pragmatic — does it serve the stakeholder's interest; moral — is someone accountable for it; cognitive — do people trust it as genuine rather than as counterfeit. AI outputs can pass pragmatic and fail moral. They routinely pass cognitive — people trust fluent language — and fail all three when examined. The dangerous case is cognitive trust without moral accountability: a doctor trusts an AI output at the bedside because it sounds right, with no mechanism for anyone to be responsible if it is wrong.
beats.visual_intent: title card: three legitimacy types — same output, different context, different moral standing

beats.narration_text: Ask for the auditor. In the terminal: claude — write a Python script that accepts an output flag and a context description, calls the Anthropic API using Suchman's three legitimacy types — pragmatic, moral, and cognitive — and returns JSON with pragmatic verdict, moral verdict, moral accountability gap, cognitive verdict as genuine or counterfeit, and what would make the cognitive verdict genuine. Print a three-cell legitimacy card. Use claude-3-5-haiku.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python script: accepts --output (AI text)
and --context. Call Anthropic API using Suchman (1995)
three legitimacy types. Return JSON: pragmatic_verdict,
moral_verdict, moral_accountability_gap,
cognitive_verdict (genuine/counterfeit),
cognitive_what_would_make_it_genuine.
Print 3-cell legitimacy card. Use claude-3-5-haiku."

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The cognitive verdict field is the key: counterfeit means the trust comes from fluency, not from traceable reasoning. Genuine means there is a mechanism — a citation, a derivation, a named expert — that grounds the trust. The distinction is not about accuracy; it is about accountability structure.

props.code:
# legitimacy_auditor.py — Claude Code output
import anthropic, json, argparse

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def audit_legitimacy(output_text, context):
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-3-5-haiku-20241022',
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':
            f'Audit this AI output for legitimacy per Suchman (1995).\n'
            f'Context: {context}\nOutput: {output_text}\n'
            f'Return JSON: pragmatic_verdict, moral_verdict,\n'
            f'moral_accountability_gap, cognitive_verdict,\n'
            f'cognitive_what_would_make_it_genuine'
        }]
    )
    # cognitive_verdict = "counterfeit" if trusted_for_fluency else "genuine"
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)

beats.narration_text: Run it on two contexts: the same AI output in a finance committee and at a bedside. The finance committee: pragmatic passes — it serves the interest; moral passes — the CFO is accountable; cognitive is traceable but not fully grounded. The bedside: pragmatic passes — it answers the query; moral fails — who is accountable if the AI is wrong at bedside? Cognitive is counterfeit — trusted for fluency, not for traceability. Same output. Different moral legitimacy.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_LegitimacyCards: 2-col 3-row matrix, finance vs bedside, moral right and cognitive right CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: The moral accountability gap field is empty in the bedside audit. Ask for a revision: expand the moral verdict to name the specific accountability gap — who would be responsible, and what mechanism would need to exist for moral legitimacy to hold.

props.command:
claude "update legitimacy_auditor.py:
  -> expand moral_accountability_gap to a full sentence
  -> name who would need to be accountable
  -> name what mechanism would establish moral legitimacy
  -> re-run both contexts"

beats.role_note: slate — fill with updated legitimacy cards
beats.narration_text: Re-run. The moral accountability gap for the bedside case now names the attending physician as the required accountable party, and names a documented review step as the mechanism. The audit is now actionable, not just descriptive.
beats.visual_intent: updated legitimacy cards with expanded moral_accountability_gap sentences for both contexts

beats.narration_text: Context changes the legitimacy structure of the same output. Pragmatic trust is about interest alignment. Moral trust requires a named accountable party. Cognitive trust requires traceability, not just fluency. An AI output can pass all three or fail all three depending entirely on context — and the audit makes that explicit.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: three legitimacy types — context changes all three verdicts; 'same output, different moral standing'

beats.narration_text: Your move: pick one high-stakes AI output you have seen this week — a recommendation, a diagnosis, a projection. Run the legitimacy audit on it for your actual context. If the cognitive verdict comes back counterfeit, the question is not whether the AI was wrong. It is whether anyone would know.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) pick a high-stakes AI output, 2) run legitimacy audit, 3) check cognitive verdict

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
